critic_raw:
This real-time sensor prediction vs. schedule-based maintenance design has a critical explicit test-set contamination and a split approach problem.

First, Section 10 states: 'As a final assurance step, the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model. This approach ensures the model's robustness against potential shifts observed during validation, providing confidence that the final training run won't require adjustment post-launch.' This is unambiguous test-set contamination. Consulting test set metrics during training — even 'briefly' and even for 'assurance' purposes — means the final model selection is informed by test set performance. The word 'briefly' does not limit the methodological severity of this violation.

Second, Section 3 uses 'stratified random sampling across the entire 36-month period' for equipment failure prediction. For time-series industrial data, temporal leakage from random splitting can cause a model trained on months 25–36 sensor readings to be evaluated on month 1–5 readings, reversing causal order. Equipment degradation trends are temporal — a model should predict failures in future windows, not past windows.

Third, the primary metric is 'Downtime Cost Reduction: (1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio).' This metric requires an 'intervention_cost_ratio' that the design says may not be available: 'If cost data is unavailable, we use F₂ score.' Having two different primary metrics depending on data availability means the experiment's primary outcome is undefined until after data collection. The design should pre-specify whether the cost metric or F₂ score will be primary.

Fourth, Section 5 correctly states all preprocessing transformers are fit on training data only — but the split in Section 3 is random, meaning 'training data' includes future observations. The preprocessing is correctly fit on training data but training data is contaminated with future data.
all_issues_raised: Test set contamination: test metrics explicitly consulted during model training
Temporal leakage: random split on 36-month time-series equipment data
Ambiguous primary metric: cost reduction vs. F2 score depending on data availability
Training data temporal contamination flows from random split

round_transcripts.critic: Explicit test set contamination (Section 10 consults test metrics during training), temporal leakage from random split, and an ambiguous primary metric that switches between cost reduction and F2 score depending on data availability.
round_transcripts.defender:
On test set contamination: Section 10's language is unambiguous — test metrics are consulted during training for 'assurance.' This is a fundamental holdout violation. Even if the consultation is 'brief' and motivated by practical robustness concerns, it means the final model is selected with test-set knowledge. The defender concedes this is a disqualifying flaw.

On temporal leakage: Equipment failure prediction is time-series data where sensor readings at time T predict failure at time T+7 days. A random split mixes future equipment states into training data. For rotating machinery with wear-based failure patterns, including future sensor readings in training reveals degradation trajectories the model should be predicting. The defender concedes temporal leakage is genuine.

On ambiguous primary metric: Specifying two potential primary metrics conditional on data availability is methodologically unsound. A pre-registration of which metric will be primary is required before data collection. The cost metric involves estimated costs; if these costs are model parameters, they are tunable and could be chosen post-hoc to favor a desired conclusion. The defender concedes this is a genuine issue.

round_transcripts.critic: All three major issues conceded by the defender. The test set contamination is explicitly documented in the design and is disqualifying.
round_transcripts.defender: All three concessions confirmed. The design has three serious methodological flaws: test set contamination, temporal leakage, and ambiguous primary metric.

round_transcripts.critic: All confirmed. The test set contamination alone is sufficient for critique wins.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed. The test set contamination explicitly documented in Section 10 invalidates the performance estimates.

all_issues_adjudicated: Test set contamination: test metrics explicitly consulted during model training (Section 10)
Temporal leakage: random split on 36-month industrial time-series data
Ambiguous primary metric: cost reduction vs. F2 score undefined before data collection
